'''Daryl Clark''' (born 10 February 1993) is an English professional [[rugby league]] footballer who plays as a {{rlp|hk}} for [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] in the [[Super League]] and [[England national rugby league team|England]] at international level.

He has previously played for [[Castleford Tigers]] and [[Warrington Wolves]] in the Super League.

==Background==
Clark was born in [[Castleford]], West Yorkshire, England. He is of [[Scottish people|Scottish]] descent.

He attended [[Airedale Academy|Airedale High School]] and played amateur rugby for Fryston Warriors.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Smith|first1=Peter|title=Castleford Tigers: Clark and Burrow set to go head to head|url=http://www.yorkshireeveningpost.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/castleford-tigers/castleford-tigers-clark-and-burrow-set-to-go-head-to-head-1-6799302|website=Yorkshire Evening Post|publisher=Johnston Press|access-date=26 December 2014|date=22 August 2014}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=Hadfield|first1=Dave|title=Daryl Clark inspired by Castleford's Challenge Cup history even as tradition points to a future elsewhere|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby/rugby-league/daryl-clark-inspired-by-castlefords-challenge-cup-history-even-as-tradition-points-to-a-future-elsewhere-9681667.html|website=The Independent|access-date=26 December 2014|date=20 August 2014}}</ref>

==Playing career==
===Castleford Tigers===
Clark started his professional career with hometown club [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]], making his debut in February 2011.<ref name="RLRKC"/> He made 21 appearances in his first season at the club, and was rewarded with a five-year contract.<ref>{{cite web|title=Castleford Tigers hooker Daryl Clark signs long-term deal|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/15381146|website=BBC Sport|access-date=24 December 2014|date=20 October 2011}}</ref>

Clark played in the [[2014 Challenge Cup Final]] defeat by the [[Leeds Rhinos]] at [[Wembley Stadium]].<ref name="Leeds lift Challenge Cup after Ryan Hall’s double stuns Castleford">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2014/aug/23/catleford-leeds-challenge-cup-final-match-report|title=Leeds lift Challenge Cup after Ryan Hall's double stuns Castleford|publisher=Guardian|date=23 August 2014|access-date=13 November 2019}}</ref>

In August 2014, it was confirmed that Clark had been signed by [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]] for a fee of £185,000.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Bower|first1=Aaron|title=Clark cost £185,000, says Warrington Wolves owner|url=http://www.totalrl.com/clark-cost-185000-says-warrington-wolves-owner/|website=Total Rugby League|publisher=League Publications|access-date=24 December 2014|date=28 August 2014}}</ref> In his final season with the club, he won the 2014 [[Man of Steel Awards|Steve Prescott Man of Steel]] award.

===Warrington Wolves===
Clark played in the [[2016 Challenge Cup final]] defeat against [[Hull F.C.]] at Wembley Stadium.<ref name="Hull FC’s Jamie Shaul’s late try takes Challenge Cup away from Warrington">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2016/aug/27/hull-warrington-challenge-cup-final-match-report|title=Hull FC's Jamie Shaul's late try takes Challenge Cup away from Warrington|publisher=Guardian|date=27 August 2016|access-date=7 November 2019}}</ref><ref name="Challenge Cup final: Hull FC 12-10 Warrington Wolves">{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/37169016|title=Challenge Cup final: Hull FC 12-10 Warrington Wolves|publisher=BBC|date=27 August 2016|access-date=7 November 2019}}</ref>

Clark played in the [[2016 Super League Grand Final]] defeat against [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] at [[Old Trafford]].<ref name="Warrington 6-12 Wigan: Super League Grand Final – as it happened!">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/live/2016/oct/08/super-league-grand-final-warrington-wigan-live|title=Warrington 6-12 Wigan: Super League Grand Final – as it happened!|publisher=Guardian|date=8 October 2016|access-date=7 November 2019}}</ref>

Clark played in the [[2018 Challenge Cup final]] defeat against [[Catalans Dragons]] at Wembley Stadium.<ref name="Catalans Dragons beat Warrington in Challenge Cup final to make history">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2018/aug/25/catalans-dragons-warrington-wolves-challenge-cup-final-match-report|title=Catalans Dragons beat Warrington in Challenge Cup final to make history|publisher=Guardian|date=25 August 2018|access-date=5 November 2019}}</ref>

Clark played in the [[2018 Super League Grand Final]] defeat against Wigan at [[Old Trafford]].<ref name="Super League Grand Final 2018: Wigan 12-4 Warrington – as it happened">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/live/2018/oct/13/super-league-grand-final-2018-wigan-warriors-v-warrington-wolves-live?page=with:block-5bc1ff43e4b05798be94cc63#liveblog-navigation|title=Super League Grand Final 2018: Wigan 12-4 Warrington – as it happened|publisher=Guardian|date=13 October 2018|access-date=5 November 2019}}</ref>

Clark played in the [[2019 Challenge Cup final]] victory over [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] at Wembley Stadium.<ref name="St Helens 4-18 Warrington RESULT: Challenge Cup Final as it happened from Wembley">{{cite web|url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/st-helens-vs-warrington-live-18996678|title=St Helens 4-18 Warrington RESULT: Challenge Cup Final as it happened from Wembley|publisher=Mirror|date=24 August 2019|access-date=1 November 2019}}</ref> He scored Warrington's third and final try and was voted man of the match, becoming only the third {{rlp|hk}} to win the [[Lance Todd Trophy]].

On 26 July 2023, Clark signed a three-year deal to join St Helens starting in 2024.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/daryl-clark-st-helens-moved-confirmed-warrington-wolves/|title=Daryl Clark: St Helens move confirmed with Warrington Wolves star signing long-term deal|website=www.loverugbyleague.com|date=26 July 2023 }}</ref> Clark played 25 games for Warrington in the 2023 Super League season as Warrington finished sixth on the table and qualified for the playoffs. Clark played in the club's elimination playoff loss against his future club St Helens.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.warringtonguardian.co.uk/sport/23827325.highs-lows-warrington-wolves-2023-season/|title=Highs and lows from Warrington Wolves' 2023 season|website=www.warringtonguardian.co.uk|date=3 October 2023 }}</ref>

===St Helens===
In round 1 of the [[2024 Super League season]], Clark made his club debut for St Helens and scored a try as they defeated the newly-promoted [[London Broncos]] 40–4.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/68304742|title=Super League: St Helens 40-4 London Broncos - Saints thrash promoted side|website=www.bbc.co.uk}}</ref> In round 14, Clark scored a [[hat-trick]] in the club's 52–6 victory over London at [[Twickenham Stoop]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/articles/cqll4833nvqo|title=Nine-try Saints hammer Broncos to return to top|website=www.bbc.co.uk}}</ref> Clark played 22 matches for St Helens in the 2024 Super League season which saw the club finish sixth on the table. Clark played in St Helens' golden-point extra-time play-off loss against Warrington.

On 8 August 2025, Clark made his 400th career appearance in St Helens' victory over [[Wakefield Trinity]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2024/sep/28/warrington-st-helens-super-league-eliminator-playoff-rugby-league-match-report/|title=
‘Soul destroying’: Warrington eliminate St Helens in golden drop goal thriller|website=www.theguardian.com}}</ref> Clark played 28 games for St Helens in the 2025 Super League season, including the club's 20–12 play-off semi-final loss against [[Hull Kingston Rovers]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/articles/c04q6rqyrv6o/|title=Retiring Waerea-Hargreaves set to miss Grand Final|website=www.bbc.co.uk}}</ref>

==International career==
In 2011, Clark was named in the [[Scotland national rugby league team|Scotland]] squad, but did not make an appearance. After playing one match for [[England Knights]] in 2012, Clark confirmed he would no longer be putting himself forward to play for Scotland, as he would prefer to play for [[England national rugby league team|England]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Wilson|first1=Zack|title=Daryl Clark rules himself out of Scotland World Cup place|url=http://www.loverugbyleague.com/news_10913-daryl-clark-rules-himself-out-of-scotland-world-cup-place.html|publisher=Love Rugby League|access-date=24 December 2014|date=7 February 2013}}</ref>

He then made his debut for England in the [[2014 Rugby League Four Nations|2014 Four Nations]] tournament in Australia.{{citation needed|date=October 2022}}

In October 2016, Clark was selected in England's [[2016 Rugby League Four Nations|2016 Four Nations]] squad. Before the tournament, England played a [[2016 Rugby League Four Nations#France vs England|test match]] against France in which Clark scored his first test try in England's 40–6 win.{{citation needed|date=October 2022}}

He was selected in the [[England 9s]] squad for the [[2019 Rugby League World Cup 9s]].<ref name="Sam Tomkins eager to lift inaugural World 9s title with England Down Under">{{cite web|url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/sam-tomkins-eager-lift-inaugural-20595184|title=Sam Tomkins eager to lift inaugural World 9s title with England Down Under|publisher=Mirror|date=16 October 2019|access-date=29 October 2019}}</ref>

He was selected in squad for the [[2019 Great Britain Lions tour]] of the Southern Hemisphere.<ref name="Zak Hardaker shock inclusion in 24-man Great Britain squad for tour of New Zealand and Papua New Guinea">{{cite web|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/rugby-league/2019/10/14/zak-hardaker-shock-inclusion-24-man-great-britain-squad-tour/|title=Zak Hardaker shock inclusion in 24-man Great Britain squad for tour of New Zealand and Papua New Guinea|publisher=Telegraph|date=14 October 2019|access-date=29 October 2019}}</ref>
